title: NOC 31204 – Kinesiologists and Other Professional Occupations in Therapy and Assessment | GTR Immigration
description: Kinesiologists and related therapy professionals play an important role in injury prevention, rehabilitation, athletic performance, and functional movement therapy across Canada. With increasing focus on preventive healthcare, workplace wellness, and sports rehabilitation, demand for these professionals continues to grow. Under NOC 31204 (TEER 1), this occupation may qualify under:
duties: Assess patients’ functional movement and physical performance
Review reports from physicians, physiotherapists, and other specialists
Develop individualized and group rehabilitation programs
Design therapy plans for musculoskeletal rehabilitation
Implement treatment sessions using movement-based therapy techniques
Monitor patient progress and prepare reports
Educate clients on injury prevention and performance enhancement
Conduct applied research in movement sciences
employment_requirements: Four-year bachelor’s degree in kinesiology, physical education, or human kinetics
Certification available through the Canadian Kinesiology Alliance
Bachelor’s degree in sports therapy or health sciences with specialization in sports injury management
Bachelor’s degree in recreation with specialization in therapeutic recreation
Membership or certification with relevant national or provincial professional associations may be required
job_outlook: While the total volume is smaller compared to other healthcare professions, demand exceeds supply slightly, especially in:
Plan Express Entry or PNP strategy
Prepare documentation for healthcare and rehabilitation roles
labour_market_demand: Professionals under NOC 31204 are employed in:
Estimated annual salary: $57,800 – $94,800 CAD, depending on specialization, province, and years of experience.
Growing awareness of injury prevention, aging population mobility support, and corporate wellness initiatives are driving demand.
Kinesiologists and related professionals benefit from:
Increasing integration into multidisciplinary healthcare teams
Strong opportunities in sports and rehabilitation sectors
Growing demand for movement and performance specialists
Employment flexibility (public, private, and self-employed options)
Access to Canada’s stable healthcare ecosystem
